analogy: Think of "Understanding Media" through the chapter's own opening images: 64 Social and Political Life Understanding Media What is your favourite TV programme? Which newspaper or magazine do you usually read? Do you surf the internet and what have you found most useful about it? Did you know that there is one word that is... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 64 Social and Political Life, 6CHAPTER, 65 Chapter 6: Understanding Media, 66 Social and Political Life. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Understanding Media is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 64 Social and Political Life Understanding Media What is your favourite TV programme?

The text explains that which newspaper or magazine do you usually read?

The text explains that do you surf the internet and what have you found most useful about it?

The text explains that did you know that there is one word that is often used to collectively refer to the radio, TV, newspapers, Internet and several other forms of communication.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will read more about the media.

The text explains that you will find out what is required to make it work, as well as the ways in which the media affects our daily lives.

The text explains that can you think of one thing that you have learnt from the media this week?

The text explains that 65 Look at the collage on the left and list six various kinds of media that you see.

The text explains that an artists impression of Gutenberg printing the first sheet of the Bible.

The text explains that ask older members of your family about what they used to listen to on the radio when there was no TV around.

The text explains that find out from them when the first TV came to your area.

The text explains that how many people in your neighbourhood use the Internet?

The text explains that list three things that you know about some other part of the world from watching television?

The text explains that everything ranging from the stall at the local fair to the programme that you see on TV can be called media.

The text explains that media is the plural form of the word medium and it describes the various ways through which we communicate in society.

The text explains that because media refers to all means of communication, everything ranging from a phone call to the evening news on TV can be called media.

The text explains that television images travel huge 65 Chapter 6: Understanding Media

The text explains that as you will read in the following chapter, advertisements are repeated in the hope that you will go out and buy what is advertised.

The text explains that it is on the basis of this information that citizens can, for example, learn how government works.

The text explains that some of the ways in which they can do this is by writing letters to the concerned minister, organising a public protest, starting a signature campaign, asking the government to rethink its programme, etc.

The text explains that chapter 6: Understanding Media The print media offers a large variety of information to suit the tastes of different readers.

The text explains that sharma, one of the factory owners said, The government says that it has done a lot to relocate us.

The text explains that the owners and workers are protesting the governments decision to close down polluting factory units.

The text explains that although the government did take this decision rather hastily, the protestors have known for quite some time that their units are not legal.

The text explains that the factory owners and workers should accept the relocation being offered by the government instead of protesting. Radhika Malik | INN

The text explains that we need to be active viewers, who question whatever we see and hear, while we may enjoy it too!

The text explains that the first is the control that the government has on the media.

The text explains that when the government prevents either a news item, or scenes from a movie, or the lyrics of a song from being shared with the larger public, this is referred to as censorship.

The text explains that there have been periods in Indian history when the government censored the media.

The text explains that 70 Social and Political Life While the government does continue to censor films, it does not really censor the medias coverage of news.

The text explains that despite the absence of censorship by the government, most newspapers nowadays still fail to provide a balanced story.

The text explains that can you think of an issue that does not seem important to you because it is never featured in the media? this despite the governments resistance by boldly declaring that colas were unsafe.

The text explains that as citizens of a democracy, the media has a very important role to play in our lives because it is through the media that we hear about issues related Chapter 6: Understanding Media

The text explains that 72 Social and Political Life to the working of the government.

The text explains that the government can, at times, prevent the media from publishing a story and this is called censorship.

The text explains that we, therefore, need to analyse the news by asking the following questions: what is the information I am learning from this report?

The text explains that social advertising Social advertisements refer to advertisements made by the Government or private agencies that have a larger message for society.

The text explains that it might help to ask the following questions a.

The text explains that censorship: This refers to the powers that government has to disallow media from publishing or showing certain stories.

The text explains that broadcast: In this chapter this word is used to refer to a TV or radio programme that is widely transmitted.

The text explains that chapter 6: Understanding Media BIG BUSINESS HOUSES READERS, VIEWERS, LISTENERS advertise their products MEDIA promotes products through advertisements People buy products seen in the media.

The text explains that markets Teachers note These two chapters focus on aspects of life and commercial cycles associated with markets.

The text explains that while some of these processes may be visible and, therefore, easily observable, there are also others that are relatively unfamiliar.

The text explains that at another level, we explore the intricate question, how do goods reach these markets? We examine how a chain of markets operates and the role of wholesale markets within this, through the case study of a wholesale vegetable market.

The text explains that we usually associate market with marketplaces, but buying and selling takes place in diverse ways and the chapter discusses how all of this falls within a larger understanding of markets.

The text explains that chapter 8 looks at how markets offer people different opportunities.

The text explains that this is done through the story of a shirt, and the chain of markets involved in the process.

The text explains that these chapters offer an opportunity of bringing in the experience of local markets for discussion in the classroom.

The text explains that a visit to a wholesale market would be of interest, and would allow the learner to find out the profit margins and details of daily earnings so that those inequalities can be directly examined.

The text explains that hence, one should allocate time for some questions, not addressed in the text, which students may wish to discuss.

The text explains that together with understanding each step of the manufacture and circulation of a shirt, we realise that some people stand to gain in the market transaction whereas others do not gain as much, or none at all.

The text explains that ways do exist, such as those of cooperative marketing, which can provide a better return to the producers.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 6: Understanding Media

prelims_traps: Do not treat 64 Social and Political Life and 6CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Understanding Media; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Understanding Media through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 64 Social and Political Life and 6CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
